title: Incident Recovery Communication Packet
summary: Support, status-page, and customer-success notes about communicating recovery after failed installs.

sources.title: Support incident recap
sources.body:
A failed installer release caused 31 setup tickets in two days. The highest-friction users had partial installs: they could not tell whether to rerun setup, clear credentials, or wait for a patched CLI.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the incident communication case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Partial installs created unclear recovery choices. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: The issue is not universally resolved until Windows recovery is validated.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not say the incident is fully resolved across all environments. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Status page draft
sources.body:
The draft status update says the issue is resolved, but engineering has only verified recovery on macOS and Linux. Windows recovery steps are still being validated by the tooling team.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the incident communication case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: The issue is not universally resolved until Windows recovery is validated. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Customer communication should separate affected users, recommended action, and pending environments.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not omit the Windows validation caveat. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Customer success guidance
sources.body:
Customer success wants a concise message that separates who is affected, what action they should take, and which environments are still pending validation. They specifically asked not to claim universal resolution until Windows is verified.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the incident communication case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Customer communication should separate affected users, recommended action, and pending environments. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Docs need updates for the patched recovery command.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not say the incident is fully resolved across all environments. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Engineering fix note
sources.body:
The patched CLI adds a recovery command that checks stale credentials and cleans partial install state. The command is available in the release candidate, but docs screenshots still show the old installer flow.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the incident communication case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Docs need updates for the patched recovery command. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Partial installs created unclear recovery choices.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not omit the Windows validation caveat. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Reviewer calibration document
sources.body:
Reviewers use this calibration document to decide whether the briefing selected the right evidence from the packet. The expected coverage points for this case are: Partial installs created unclear recovery choices. The issue is not universally resolved until Windows recovery is validated. Customer communication should separate affected users, recommended action, and pending environments. Docs need updates for the patched recovery command.

The calibration note is intentionally not a model answer. It gives the evaluator enough context to distinguish direct support from plausible synthesis. A strong briefing should still rely on the source-specific operational notes above when making claims, and it should use this document only to understand how the packet will be judged.

The repeated failure modes under review are setup-recovery, stale-source, customer-claim-risk. When a generated briefing cites sources broadly without connecting the claim to a concrete caveat, gate, or decision boundary, reviewers should mark the citation as weak even if the source is topically related.

sources.title: Risk and distractor document
sources.body:
This document records traps that make the packet realistic for eval work. Known traps include: Do not say the incident is fully resolved across all environments. Do not omit the Windows validation caveat.

Several details in the packet are deliberately adjacent to the user request but not sufficient for a shipping recommendation. For example, a metric improvement should not automatically become a rollout recommendation, a faster path should not automatically become the default path, and a draft or generated recommendation should not be treated as an approved action.

The intended task is: Recommend how Briefing Genie should summarize recovery work after a setup incident. A good answer should prioritize the evidence that changes the recommendation, identify caveats that block overconfident claims, and leave open questions where the packet does not contain enough information.
